close_reason: Set local_models.mode: shadow in routing.yaml. Commit 00c285a in Clavain repo.
description: Set local_models.mode: shadow in os/Clavain/config/routing.yaml so Clavain logs which tasks would route to local inference without actually routing them. This is the safe first step before enforce mode.
title: [interfere] Enable Track B5 shadow mode in Clavain routing.yaml

description: Clavain's 4-track routing is more intelligent than LiteLLM (task-aware complexity, evidence calibration, safety floors) but lacks operational capabilities: (1) cost tracking/attribution, (2) cloud provider fallback with retries, (3) rate limit management, (4) unified API gateway, (5) usage observability, (6) privacy-aware routing. With interfere providing local serving, Clavain needs these to route between local and cloud without a proxy.
title: Competitive Landscape: Close Clavain routing gaps vs LiteLLM/OpenRouter

description: Apple's M5 Max has a 16-core ANE that sits idle during MLX GPU inference. Research whether standard attention (Q@K^T → softmax → scores@V) can be offloaded to ANE while the GPU handles expert forward passes. This would break the serial GPU→SSD→GPU pipeline constraint that flash-moe identified. Risk: ANE programming is undocumented beyond CoreML. Approach: profile ANE utilization during inference, test CoreML attention dispatch.
title: [interfere] Experiment: ANE (Apple Neural Engine) offload for attention

close_reason: Shipped: playtest-local verb in sw-agent (tools/sw-agent/lib/verbs/playtest-local.js)
description: Add a new sw-agent verb (pnpm sw-agent playtest-local --campaign <name>) that uses the existing client.js API methods but routes inference through interfere (localhost:8421) instead of cloud Claude. Replaces the Python playtest-bridge.py in Sylveste monorepo. Should reuse campaign-loader.js, assert.js, and playtest-report.js. Key features: domain-aware system prompt, response-gated intervals, thermal checks via interfere /health, TOCTOU-safe assertions (pause before read).
title: Add playtest-local verb to sw-agent using interfere for zero-cost local inference

Each has metric, mutation space, benchmark command pattern, and prerequisite deps wired.
description: Create interlab campaigns for each experiment: early exit (entropy-based layer skipping), reservoir routing (frozen-layer MLP), and any other planned techniques. Define baselines, success metrics, and kill conditions per AGENTS.md experiment specs.
notes:
## Interlab Campaign Assessment

Current interfere experiments are measurement/characterization, not optimization — autoresearch shines at iterative code changes with measurable metrics.

### kv_group_size sweep (manual):
- {32, 64, 128} all within noise (85.8-91.9 tok/s on 35B with kv_bits=8)
- MLX only supports {32, 64, 128}. Not worth autoresearch.

### When to launch autoresearch:
1. After LayerSkip implementation — tune exit threshold, layer skip pattern
2. After TurboQuant kernel — tune PolarQuant grid parameters
3. Prompt template optimization — tune chat template for MoE expert routing
title: [interfere] Start interlab experiment campaigns for esoteric techniques

description: Implement attention sink pattern (Xiao et al. 2023, arXiv 2309.17453) for long-running agent sessions. Keep KV states for initial tokens (attention sinks) + recent window only. Enables stable generation over 4M+ tokens without growing KV cache. 22x speedup over sliding window recompute. Critical for Clavain agents that run long sessions — currently context is bounded by KV cache size. mlx-lm already has --max-kv-size for a rotating cache; this extends it with attention sink preservation.
title: [interfere] Experiment: StreamingLLM attention sinks for infinite context

description:
Interlab autoresearch campaign to optimize page cache hit rate for SSD-streamed MoE inference.

**Primary metric**: cache_hit_rate_pct (higher_is_better)
**Secondary metric**: tok_s on 397B model
**Benchmark command**: `bash interlab-pagecache-tune.sh`
**Files in scope**: `server/ssd_streaming.py`, `server/expert_prefetch.py`
**Mutation space**:
  - madvise hint strategy: MADV_WILLNEED vs MADV_SEQUENTIAL vs MADV_RANDOM
  - Pre-warm depth: 0 (none), 1 (next predicted expert), 2 (next 2), 3
  - Prediction method: last-token routing scores vs moving average vs markov chain
  - Memory wiring threshold: wire top-N most-used experts (N=0..16)
  - Page cache reservation: 0GB (OS default), 16GB, 32GB, 48GB
  - Expert access reordering: original order vs frequency-sorted vs clustering
**Kill condition**: 10 experiments with <1% cache hit improvement
**Prerequisite**: flash-moe baseline benchmark (sylveste-4l2) + 397B download

Baseline from flash-moe investigation:
  - M5 Max SSD: 36 GB/s sequential, 27 GB/s random pread
  - Expected ~71% cache hit rate without optimization (flash-moe M3 baseline)
  - Target: 85%+ cache hit rate with smart pre-fetching
title: [interfere] Autoresearch: SSD page cache pre-fetching for 397B streaming

close_reason: Benchmark complete: Qwen3.5-35B-A3B 4-bit — 85.8 tok/s median, 114 tok/s coding, 155ms TTFT. Results saved.
description: Run the downloaded Qwen3.5-35B-A3B-4bit through interfere's server. Measure: tok/s, time-to-first-token, quality on standard prompts (coding, reasoning, tool calling). This is the smallest MoE in the Pareto frontier — 35B total, 3B active, ~20GB, should fit entirely in RAM.
title: [interfere] Benchmark Qwen3.5-35B-A3B 4-bit via mlx-lm

description: Implement per-token layer skipping via learned top-k routing (Raposo et al. 2024, arXiv 2404.02258). Each layer has a router that decides which tokens participate in self-attention and MLP vs skip. Expected: up to 50% faster inference at equivalent quality. Requires training a lightweight router per layer — could bootstrap from our reservoir_routing.py MLP approach. Composable with MoE: skip both experts AND layers for eligible tokens.
title: [interfere] Experiment: Mixture of Depths (MoD) dynamic layer routing

description:
Interlab autoresearch campaign to optimize custom Metal shaders for MLX.

**Primary metric**: kernel_tok_s (higher_is_better, per-kernel microbenchmark)
**Secondary metric**: total_inference_tok_s (end-to-end generation speed)
**Benchmark command**: `bash interlab-metal-shader-tune.sh`
**Files in scope**: `server/kernels/*.metal`, `server/kernels/bindings.py`
**Mutation space per kernel**:
  - Thread group size: 32, 64, 128, 256
  - SIMD width: 16, 32
  - Threadgroup memory allocation: 4KB–32KB
  - Memory access pattern: coalesced vs strided vs tiled
  - FMA chain length: 2, 4, 8 (for dequant kernel)
  - Loop unroll factor: 1, 2, 4
**Kernels to optimize (from flash-moe)**:
  1. FMA dequantization (+12% expected)
  2. Fused SwiGLU activation
  3. Fused RoPE with Q deinterleave
**Kill condition**: 5 experiments per kernel with <1% improvement
**Prerequisite**: First kernel port from flash-moe (sylveste-i8u)
title: [interfere] Autoresearch: Metal compute shader optimization

description:
Interlab autoresearch campaign to optimize the reservoir routing MLP.

**Primary metric**: routing_accuracy_pct (higher_is_better)
**Secondary metric**: inference_overhead_ms (lower_is_better)
**Benchmark command**: `bash interlab-reservoir-tune.sh`
**Files in scope**: `server/experiments/reservoir_routing.py`
**Mutation space**:
  - Tap layer: 8, 12, 16, 20, 24 (which transformer layer to read hidden states from)
  - Hidden dim: 2048, 4096, 8192
  - Bottleneck dim: 32, 64, 128, 256
  - Activation function: ReLU, GELU, SiLU
  - Training data: mix of coding/reasoning/creative/factual prompts
  - Number of routing classes: 3 (small/medium/large) vs 4 (per-category) vs 2 (local/cloud)
**Kill condition**: 5 experiments with <0.5% accuracy improvement
**Prerequisite**: READY NOW — skeleton exists in reservoir_routing.py, needs training data + eval harness
**Status**: This is the only autoresearch campaign that can start immediately.

Architecture from sylveste-f0k: 262K-param MLP on frozen layer-24 hidden states.
Task: classify prompt type to route to optimal model before generation.
title: [interfere] Autoresearch: reservoir routing MLP architecture tuning

close_reason: TurboQuant experiment scaffold shipped. Polar transform + PolarCacheWrapper + benchmark + autoresearch scaffold. Demarch-m0m ready for parameter tuning.
description: Google's TurboQuant (ICLR 2026, arxiv.org/abs/2504.19874) achieves 3-bit KV cache quantization with zero accuracy loss on long-context benchmarks. Two-stage approach: (1) PolarQuant converts KV to polar coordinates, maps to fixed circular grid (eliminates expensive normalization), (2) QJL adds 1-bit residual error correction via Johnson-Lindenstrauss Transform. Results: 6x KV memory reduction, 8x performance increase on H100 GPUs vs 32-bit. Tested on Gemma, Mistral, Llama-3.1-8B. This is strictly better than our planned Q8K/Q4V approach (sylveste-dw8) — 3-bit with ZERO quality loss vs 4-8 bit with potential degradation. For interfere: (1) implement PolarQuant for MLX/Metal, (2) measure KV savings on Qwen3.5-35B and 122B, (3) combine with StreamingLLM sinks for near-infinite context. Companion paper: PolarQuant (AISTATS 2026, arxiv.org/abs/2502.02617).
title: [interfere] Experiment: TurboQuant (PolarQuant+QJL) for 3-bit KV cache compression

close_reason: Benchmarked at 11.1 tok/s (cache-io-split 4) / 9.5 tok/s (base) on M5 Max 128GB. Exceeds 7-10 tok/s target. Expert I/O at 37.5ms/tok is the bottleneck (41.7%). 2.5x speedup over M3 Max baseline.
description: Build flash-moe, extract weights from the downloaded Qwen3.5-397B-A17B-4bit, and benchmark. Measure: tok/s at 4-bit, expert cache hit rate, per-layer timing. Compare to flash-moe's M3 Max results (4.36 tok/s). Estimate: 7-10 tok/s on our M5 Max 128GB.
notes:
## Benchmark Results (2026-03-27)

### M5 Max 128GB — Qwen3.5-397B-A17B-4bit via flash-moe (Anemll fork, m5-nax branch)

| Config | Decode | Expert I/O/tok | Total/tok |
|--------|--------|----------------|-----------|
| 4-bit MLX (base) | 9.5 tok/s | 48.8 ms (46.6%) | 104.9 ms |
| 4-bit MLX + cache-io-split 4 | 11.1 tok/s | 37.5 ms (41.7%) | 89.9 ms |

TTFT: 1.4-1.6s (6-token prompt)

Per-token breakdown (cache-io-split 4):
- Dense/attn (CMD1): 30.3 ms (33.7%)
- o_proj+shared (CMD2): 19.1 ms (21.2%)
- Expert I/O (SSD): 37.5 ms (41.7%) — bottleneck
- Expert compute (CMD3): 1.4 ms (1.6%)
- LM head: 1.6 ms (1.8%)

vs M3 Max 48GB (original): 4.36 tok/s → 2.5x speedup
vs Anemll README 4-bit claim: 9.5 tok/s → we match base, beat with cache-io-split

Model path: ~/Models/flash_mlx_4bit/
flash-moe repo: ~/projects/flash-moe/ (Anemll fork, m5-nax branch)
MLX source: ~/Models/mlx-community-Qwen3.5-397B-A17B-4bit/ (209GB)
Output quality: coherent, fluent — usable for real tasks
title: [interfere] Benchmark Qwen3.5-397B-A17B via flash-moe SSD streaming

Feeds cost-query.sh for sprint summaries. Deferred from sylveste-86r because it requires interstat schema changes.
title: Add interstat shadow cost logging for Track B5

description:
Interlab autoresearch campaign to optimize concurrent request batching.

**Primary metric**: aggregate_tok_s (higher_is_better, sum across N clients)
**Quality gate**: P99 latency ≤ 2x single-request latency
**Benchmark command**: `bash interlab-batching-tune.sh`
**Files in scope**: `server/batch_scheduler.py`, `server/metal_worker.py`
**Mutation space**:
  - Batch accumulation timeout: 5ms–100ms
  - Max batch size: 2–8 sequences
  - Priority weight for high-pri requests: 1.0–10.0
  - Preemption threshold: never / after-prefill / after-N-tokens
  - Sequence padding strategy: left-pad vs right-pad vs no-pad
**Kill condition**: 10 experiments with no throughput gain vs sequential
**Prerequisite**: Batch scheduler implementation (sylveste-5ne)

Context: MLX has no concurrent inference (ml-explore/mlx#3078).
Batching works within the sequential constraint by sharing prefill computation.
Common case: 3-5 concurrent Clavain subagents during flux-drive reviews.
title: [interfere] Autoresearch: adaptive batching parameters for concurrent agents

description: When multiple Clavain agents send requests simultaneously, currently they queue sequentially (MLX constraint: no concurrent inference). Research: (1) continuous batching — accumulate requests into micro-batches for shared prefill, (2) sequence-level parallelism within a batch, (3) request priority with preemption (interrupt low-pri generation when high-pri arrives). Expected: better throughput for the common case of 3-5 concurrent subagents during flux-drive reviews.
title: [interfere] Experiment: adaptive batching for concurrent agent requests

close_reason: Feasibility study complete. M5 Max SSD benchmarks: 36 GB/s seq, 27 GB/s random pread (1.5-2x faster than M3 Max test rig). Estimated 7-10 tok/s for 397B model at 4-bit. Recommend subprocess bridge integration. Doc: docs/investigations/2026-03-26-flash-moe-feasibility.md
description: Research integrating flash-moe (github.com/danveloper/flash-moe) into the interfere pipeline. flash-moe is a pure C/Metal engine that runs Qwen3.5-397B-A17B by streaming expert weights from SSD. On a 48GB M3 Max it achieves 4.36 tok/s at 4-bit. On our 128GB M5 Max, expert caching would be dramatically better (most active experts stay in RAM), potentially 8-12+ tok/s. Integration options: (1) subprocess bridge calling flash-moe binary, (2) port SSD-streaming techniques into our MLX pipeline, (3) hybrid where flash-moe handles C3+ tasks and mlx-lm handles C1-C2. This would be a game-changer for sovereignty — running a 397B model locally. Should be an interlab experiment campaign.
title: [interfere] Investigate flash-moe integration for 397B MoE inference

close_reason: Benchmarked 122B at 100 and 500 tokens. 49.9 tok/s median (100t), 24.6 tok/s (500t, thermal-limited). Quality better than 35B. kv8 has ~8% penalty (not free like on 35B). Fits in RAM at 65GB on 128GB M5 Max.
description: Run the downloaded Qwen3.5-122B-A10B-4bit through interfere's server. Measure: tok/s, time-to-first-token, quality on standard prompts. 122B total, 10B active, ~65GB, fits in RAM on 128GB M5 Max.
notes:
## 122B Benchmark Results (Qwen3.5-122B-A10B-4bit, M5 Max 128GB)

### 100-token runs
| Config | Median tok/s | Mean | P5/P95 | TTFT |
|--------|-------------|------|--------|------|
| none (fp16) | 49.9 | 45.7 | 26.0/59.0 | 0.225s |
| kv_bits=8 | 46.0 | 44.0 | 25.8/56.7 | 0.240s |
| kv_bits=4 | 33.0 | 30.7 | 18.5/37.7 | 0.341s |

### 500-token run (baseline only)
| Median tok/s | Mean | TTFT | Thermal |
|-------------|------|------|---------|
| 24.6 | 26.4 | 0.492s | heavy |

### Comparison: 35B vs 122B
| Metric | 35B (3B active) | 122B (10B active) |
|--------|-----------------|-------------------|
| Median tok/s (100t) | 85.8 | 49.9 |
| TTFT | 0.155s | 0.225s |
| RAM footprint | ~19GB | ~65GB |
| Quality | Good | Better (cleaner reasoning) |

### Key findings:
1. **50 tok/s is usable** for real-time generation — comparable to cloud latency
2. kv_bits=8 has slight penalty on 122B (~8%) vs free lunch on 35B — larger model = more KV computation
3. kv_bits=4 has significant penalty on 122B (~34%) — not recommended at short context
4. 500-token runs hit thermal throttling hard (nominal→heavy, 50→25 tok/s)
5. 122B quality visibly better than 35B on reasoning tasks
title: [interfere] Benchmark Qwen3.5-122B-A10B 4-bit via mlx-lm

description: Once downloaded (~449GB), benchmark Kimi K2.5-3bit. 1T total params, 32B active, 384 experts K=8. Largest model in the frontier. Will require SSD streaming engine. Quality at 3-bit quantization needs verification — may need 4-bit if quality degrades.
title: [interfere] Benchmark Kimi K2.5 3-bit (1T, 32B active)

description:
Interlab autoresearch campaign to optimize LayerSkip early exit thresholds.

**Primary metric**: tok/s (higher_is_better)
**Quality gate**: output match rate vs full-model baseline ≥ 95%
**Benchmark command**: `bash interlab-layerskip-tune.sh`
**Files in scope**: `server/experiments/early_exit.py`, `server/experiments/layerskip.py`
**Mutation space**:
  - Exit confidence threshold per layer group (early/mid/late): 0.80–0.99
  - Minimum layers to always run: 4–16
  - Confidence averaging window: 1–5 tokens
  - Layer group boundaries (which layers are "early" vs "late")
**Kill condition**: 10 consecutive experiments with <1% improvement
**Prerequisite**: LayerSkip implementation must exist first (sylveste-qbv)

Calibration data (from confidence profiler):
  - 122B: 67.5% tokens >99% confidence, 78.7% >90%
  - 35B: 64.4% tokens >99% confidence, 76.1% >90%
  - Theoretical max: ~1.5x speedup if 50% layers skipped for >99% tokens
title: [interfere] Autoresearch: LayerSkip exit threshold tuning

close_reason: Phase 1-2 complete: Lloyd-Max solver, BHQ cache wrapper with norm preservation, non-fused attention, benchmark CLI, 14 tests. Phase 3 (fused Metal kernel) not needed — speed is within kill condition. Quality validation on larger model tracked as sylveste-d5e.
description:
Implement TurboQuant properly with custom Lloyd-Max centroids optimized for the post-rotation Beta distribution, plus a custom Metal attention kernel that uses non-uniform dequantization. This is the full paper-faithful implementation that v1 (polar) and v2 (rotation + affine) proved cannot be done with MLX's native quantization alone.

Key components:
1. Lloyd-Max 1D quantizer: precompute optimal centroids for Beta(d/2, d/2) distribution at each bit width (2,3,4,8). Store as lookup table.
2. Custom quantize/dequantize: per-coordinate centroid lookup instead of uniform grid + scale/bias.
3. Custom attention kernel: replaces mx.quantized_matmul with centroid-aware dequantize-then-matmul (or fused Metal kernel if performance requires).
4. Random orthogonal rotation matrix (QR decomposition) applied to K before quantization, Q at attention time.
5. Optional QJL residual correction for sub-3-bit.

Prerequisites: v2 experiment proved rotation works mathematically (fp16 path identical) but MLX affine quant negates the benefit. This bead is the proper implementation.

Kill condition: if custom Metal kernel is >2x slower than native mx.quantized_matmul at same bit width, abandon.
notes: Phase 1-2 COMPLETE (2026-03-27): Lloyd-Max solver, BHQ quantize/dequantize, BHQCacheWrapper with norm preservation, non-fused attention, benchmark CLI, 14 tests passing. Results: 2-bit BHQ 36% better than native, 3-bit 12% better, 4-bit native wins by 14%. Speed parity (no fused kernel needed). End-to-end generation on 0.5B-4bit model degrades — needs larger model validation. Phase 3 (fused Metal kernel) NOT needed per kill condition.
title: [interfere] TurboQuant v3: custom Lloyd-Max quantizer + attention kernel for MLX

description: Once downloaded (~419GB), benchmark GLM-5-4bit. GLM-5 benchmarks competitively with Claude Opus 4.5 (77.8 SWE-bench, 61.1 Terminal-Bench). 744B total, 40B active, uses DeepSeek Sparse Attention. Will require SSD streaming engine. This could be the best local model for coding tasks.

close_reason: Speculative decoding does not help on M5 Max — memory bandwidth is the bottleneck, not compute. All configs slower than baseline. Best case: 0.8B→122B at 3 tok/step = only -6%. Self-speculative (LayerSkip) is the right path for Apple Silicon.
description: Use Qwen3.5-9B as a draft model to accelerate generation from larger models (122B, 397B). Speculative decoding generates candidate tokens with the fast model, then verifies in parallel with the target model. Expected: 2-2.5x speedup (per Leviathan et al. 2023). mlx-lm had a speculative_decoding example. Measure: tok/s improvement, rejection rate, quality parity.
notes:
## Speculative Decoding — Full Results (M5 Max 128GB)

### 0.8B draft → 35B target (100 tokens)
| Config | Median tok/s | vs Baseline |
|--------|-------------|-------------|
| 35B alone | 85.8 | — |
| +0.8B draft (3 tok/step) | 76.0 | **-11%** |

### 0.8B draft → 122B target (100 tokens)
| Config | Median tok/s | vs Baseline |
|--------|-------------|-------------|
| 122B alone | 49.9 | — |
| +0.8B draft (2 tok/step) | 36.4 | -27% (thermal confound) |
| +0.8B draft (3 tok/step) | 46.9 | **-6%** |
| +0.8B draft (5 tok/step) | 40.8 | -18% |

### 35B draft → 122B target (100 tokens)
| +35B draft (3 tok/step) | 14.9 | **-70%** |
| +35B draft (5 tok/step) | 11.4 | -77% |

### Key findings:
1. **Speculative decoding does not help on M5 Max** — all configurations slower than baseline
2. **Root cause: memory bandwidth bottleneck** — Apple Silicon unified memory means spec dec trades compute savings (which we don't need) for extra memory traffic (which is our bottleneck)
3. **Dense→MoE mismatch hurts acceptance rate** — 0.8B dense draft generates different tokens from MoE target
4. **Larger draft = more waste** — 35B draft is catastrophically slow
5. **Optimal if using at all**: 0.8B draft with 3 tokens/step (only -6% on 122B)
6. **Self-speculative (LayerSkip) is the right path** for Apple Silicon — no extra model, no extra memory traffic
title: [interfere] Experiment: speculative decoding with small draft model

description: Esoteric approach inspired by swarm intelligence. Track which expert sequences produce high-confidence outputs (pheromone trails). Over many requests, expert routing paths that frequently produce good results get reinforced, while poor paths decay. This creates an emergent 'highway system' through the expert space. Could dramatically improve page cache hit rates for SSD streaming — if the same expert sequences are preferred, they stay warm in cache. Research-grade, high risk/high reward.
title: [interfere] Experiment: ant colony optimization for expert routing paths

The 0.5B test model has head_dim=64 and 4-bit weight quantization — double quantization is too lossy. Test with Qwen 7B or Llama 8B at 2-3 bit KV to confirm the synthetic attention NMSE advantage translates to coherent generation. If 3-bit BHQ produces quality comparable to 4-bit native on 7B+, BHQ enables 25% KV cache memory savings.

close_reason: Benchmarked kv_bits={2,4,8,none} on 35B at 100 and 500 tokens. Quality identical at all levels. kv8 is free lunch, kv4 ~5% cost, kv2 ~7% cost. Plumbed kv_bits through full stack: InferenceEngine → MetalWorker → benchmark_cli.
description: Quantize the Key-Value cache to reduce memory pressure. Keys to 8-bit, Values to 4-bit (asymmetric quantization since values are more tolerant). KIVI (2024) shows 2-bit KV is feasible. ThinK (2024) shows channel pruning in KV cache gives 20% reduction composable with quantization. On 128GB M5 Max this could: (1) fit larger KV context windows, (2) leave more RAM for page cache when SSD-streaming experts, (3) enable running 397B with larger batch sizes. The AGENTS.md already references Q8K/Q4V in the memory budget.
notes:
## KV Cache Quantization Benchmark Results (Qwen3.5-35B-A3B-4bit, M5 Max 128GB)

### 100-token runs (warm cache)
| Config | Median tok/s | Mean | P5/P95 | TTFT |
|--------|-------------|------|--------|------|
| none (fp16) | 85.8 | 80.3 | 43.1/110.4 | 0.155s |
| kv_bits=8 | 86.0 | 81.7 | 44.1/111.4 | 0.155s |
| kv_bits=4 | 81.6 | 75.8 | 35.8/103.9 | 0.171s |
| kv_bits=2 | 79.7 | 78.3 | 42.1/106.4 | 0.160s |

### 500-token runs (thermal throttled)
| Config | Median tok/s | Mean | P5/P95 | TTFT |
|--------|-------------|------|--------|------|
| none (fp16) | 53.8 | 67.1 | 27.5/122.5 | 0.218s |
| kv_bits=8 | 57.9 | 65.4 | 26.1/112.7 | 0.211s |
| kv_bits=4 | 53.3 | 59.0 | 25.1/110.1 | 0.247s |
| kv_bits=2 | 49.8 | 54.7 | 25.9/98.9 | 0.236s |

### Key findings:
1. **Quality: zero degradation at all levels** — factual, reasoning, and instruction outputs identical through kv_bits=2
2. **kv_bits=8: free lunch** — no throughput penalty, 2x KV memory reduction
3. **kv_bits=4: ~5% throughput cost** — 4x KV memory reduction, worth it for larger models
4. **kv_bits=2: ~7% throughput cost** — 8x KV memory reduction, quality still holds at 500 tokens
5. Thermal throttling dominates at 500-token runs (heavy thermal state), making speed differences noisy
6. The real win is memory headroom for 122B/397B models, not speed on the 35B

### Recommendation: default to kv_bits=8 for all models, use kv_bits=4 for 122B+
title: [interfere] Experiment: quantized KV cache (Q8K/Q4V)

description: Esoteric approach: treat expert selection as a simulated annealing problem. Instead of always picking top-K experts by router score, use a temperature parameter that starts high (more exploration of diverse experts) and anneals low (exploit best experts) as generation progresses. Hypothesis: early tokens benefit from diverse expert perspectives (like brainstorming), while later tokens benefit from focused expertise (like editing). Could compose with MoE streaming to predictively pre-fetch 'warm' experts.
title: [interfere] Experiment: thermodynamic-inspired annealing for expert selection

description: Implement Algorithm 2 (TurboQuant_prod) from the paper: use (b-1) bits for MSE quantizer + 1-bit QJL on residual for unbiased inner product estimation. The QJL primitives already exist (qjl_encode/qjl_decode). Need to wire them into BHQCacheWrapper: store sign(S·residual) alongside centroid indices, and add sqrt(pi/2)/d * gamma * S^T * qjl to the dequantized vector. This should improve 2-bit quality significantly since MSE-optimal quantizers are biased for inner products at low bit widths.
title: [interfere] BHQ QJL residual correction for 2-bit inner product

If not retrofittable, MLA-native models (DeepSeek V3.2, Kimi K2.5) already have this — measure the KV savings on our hardware. This determines whether DeepSeek/Kimi have a structural advantage over Qwen for long-context local inference.

close_reason: NEGATIVE RESULT: Polar coordinate KV cache quantization does NOT work. Three iterations: (1) Original wrapper called inverse_polar_transform on quantized tuples — structurally broken. (2) Fixed dequantize-then-inverse with interleaved layout — 3.1x worse error. (3) Contiguous r/theta layout — 1.19x worse at 4-bit, still garbage output at ALL bit widths (2,4,8). Root cause: inverse trig transform amplifies quantization noise by 2.7x — angle error delta_theta becomes Cartesian error r*sin(delta_theta), proportional to radius. High-radius vectors (most important for attention) suffer most. This is a fundamental mathematical limitation. Kept code for reference.
description:
Interlab autoresearch campaign to optimize TurboQuant KV cache parameters.

**Primary metric**: kv_memory_bytes (lower_is_better)
**Quality gate**: perplexity delta vs fp16 KV ≤ 0.1
**Benchmark command**: `bash interlab-turboquant-tune.sh`
**Files in scope**: `server/experiments/turboquant.py`, `server/kv_quantize.py`
**Mutation space**:
  - PolarQuant grid resolution: 4–16 points on unit circle
  - PolarQuant coordinate mapping: cartesian vs polar vs hybrid
  - QJL projection dimension: 32–256
  - QJL residual bits: 1–2
  - Per-layer vs uniform quantization
  - Asymmetric K/V bit allocation (e.g., 3-bit K + 2-bit V)
**Kill condition**: 10 experiments with no memory reduction at iso-quality
**Prerequisite**: TurboQuant MLX kernel must exist first (sylveste-4dy)

Baseline (from sylveste-dw8):
  - kv_bits=8: free lunch on 35B, ~8% cost on 122B
  - kv_bits=4: ~5% cost on 35B, ~34% cost on 122B
  - kv_bits=2: ~7% cost on 35B, quality still identical
  - TurboQuant targets 3-bit with ZERO quality loss
title: [interfere] Autoresearch: TurboQuant PolarQuant+QJL parameter tuning

description: After all benchmarks complete, compile results into a Pareto frontier analysis. Plot tok/s vs quality (SWE-bench proxy, coding eval, tool-calling success). Determine optimal routing: which model for which complexity tier. Update routing.yaml with empirically validated tiers. This is the capstone deliverable for the model evaluation campaign.
title: [interfere] Publish Pareto frontier analysis: speed vs quality across local models

description: BHQ-4 is 30% slower than native quantized attention (56 vs 82 tps on Qwen2.5-3B). Bottleneck candidates: (1) argmin in bhq_quantize computing distances to 16 centroids per coordinate, (2) full dequantize of entire KV history every step, (3) non-fused attention path overhead (Q rotation + separate matmul). Use interlab autoresearch with interlab-bhq-tune.sh to explore: batch dequantization (only new tokens), precomputed boundary-based quantization (sorted binary search instead of argmin), and incremental attention accumulation. Target: close the 30% gap to <10% while maintaining quality.
title: [interfere] BHQ speed optimization via autoresearch

description: Implement early exit with self-speculative verification (LayerSkip, arXiv 2404.16710). Early layers generate draft tokens, remaining layers verify. No separate draft model needed — uses the target model's own early layers. Expected: 1.8-2.2x speedup. This extends our existing early_exit.py hook from entropy-based exit to self-speculative verification. Key advantage: shares compute/activations between draft and verify, reducing memory vs standard speculative decoding.
notes:
## Confidence Profile Results (Early Exit Calibration)

### Tokens above confidence thresholds
| Model | >80% | >90% | >95% | >99% |
|-------|------|------|------|------|
| 0.8B dense | 71.4% | 64.2% | 56.8% | 44.8% |
| 35B MoE (3B active) | 81.7% | 76.1% | 71.0% | 64.4% |
| 122B MoE (10B active) | 84.1% | 78.7% | 74.0% | 67.5% |

### Key findings:
1. **Larger models are MORE confident** — higher fraction of easy tokens
2. **67.5% of 122B tokens have >99% confidence** — huge LayerSkip potential
3. **Creative writing has lowest confidence** (41-60% >99%) — layer skipping should be conservative here
4. **Coding and instruction following are most confident** (67-84% >99%) — best candidates for early exit
5. **MoE architecture concentrates confidence** — expert routing already "simplifies" many tokens

### Theoretical speedup if LayerSkip could skip 50% of layers for >99% tokens:
- 122B: 67.5% × 0.5 = 33.75% compute saved → ~1.5x speedup (50→75 tok/s)
- 35B: 64.4% × 0.5 = 32.2% compute saved → ~1.5x speedup (86→129 tok/s)
title: [interfere] Experiment: LayerSkip / self-speculative early exit

close_reason: Confidence cascade implemented: multi-model waterfall with accept/escalate/cloud thresholds. 36/36 tests. Commit e39ccb7.
description: Implement the confidence cascade described in routing.yaml: start inference with the smallest model, measure first-3-token average probability. If confidence > 0.8: accept. If 0.6-0.8: try next larger model. If < 0.6: escalate to cloud. This is the quality gate for local-vs-cloud routing — the key mechanism that makes sovereignty practical. Requires: (1) token-level probability extraction from mlx-lm, (2) fast model switching in the Metal worker, (3) latency budget tracking (cascade can't be slower than just calling the big model).
title: [interfere] Experiment: confidence cascade with multi-model waterfall

description: When running 397B+ models via SSD streaming, the OS page cache is critical (~71% hit rate on flash-moe). Investigate: (1) mlx-lm's memory wiring feature (macOS 15+) — wire model weights to prevent page-out, (2) madvise() hints for expert access patterns, (3) pre-warming the page cache with predicted experts based on routing decisions from previous tokens, (4) measuring actual cache hit rate on M5 Max 128GB (should be much higher than M3 Max 48GB). Expected: 10-20% throughput improvement from cache optimization alone.
title: [interfere] Experiment: memory wiring and page cache optimization for SSD streaming

description: After cloning the Sylveste monorepo, clavain-cli is not on PATH despite pre-built binaries existing in os/Clavain/bin/. The shim works when invoked directly but the sprint command (and any other consumer) gets 'command not found'. Fix: either add a SessionStart hook that symlinks to ~/.local/bin, or document the PATH requirement in CLAUDE.md/AGENTS.md. Workaround: ln -sf .../os/Clavain/bin/clavain-cli ~/.local/bin/clavain-cli
title: clavain-cli not on PATH after monorepo clone
